citations.note: Wikipedia Roofline model article derives this directly from Williams, Waterman, Patterson (CACM 2009, doi:10.1145/1498765.1498785); notation π = peak FLOP/s, β = peak bandwidth (bytes/s), I = arithmetic intensity (FLOP/byte). The CACM paper expresses the same bound as: Attainable GFlops/sec = Min(Peak Floating Point Performance, Peak Memory Bandwidth × Operational Intensity).

citations.value: bounds attainable performance as the minimum of peak compute and memory-bandwidth × arithmetic intensity
citations.note: Canonical source: Samuel Williams, Andrew Waterman, David Patterson, 'Roofline: An Insightful Visual Performance Model for Floating-Point Programs and Multicore Architectures', Communications of the ACM, vol. 52 no. 4, April 2009.

Williams et al. (CACM 2009) define operational intensity as FLOPs per byte of DRAM traffic (not cache traffic). The exact quote: 'operational intensity... is the ratio of floating-point operations to DRAM traffic'.

citations.claim: ridge point formula and memory-bound vs compute-bound criterion
citations.value: I_ridge = π / β; memory-bound iff I < π/β, compute-bound iff I ≥ π/β
citations.note: Wikipedia Roofline article (sourced from Williams et al. CACM 2009) defines the ridge point as the intersection of the bandwidth roof and compute roof: 'the minimum arithmetic intensity required to achieve peak performance'. The formula I_ridge = π/β follows algebraically from setting β×I = π.

Original proof: John D.C. Little, 'A Proof for the Queuing Formula: L = λW', Operations Research vol. 9 no. 3, 1961, pp. 383-387. Valid for any stable system regardless of arrival or service distribution; requires only stationarity. Wikipedia article and INFORMS 50th anniversary paper (doi:10.1287/opre.1110.0940) both confirm this.

citations.claim: throughput = concurrency / latency (rearrangement of Little's Law); batching raises arithmetic intensity
citations.value: throughput = L / W; batch size B multiplies arithmetic intensity of weight-reusing kernels by B
citations.note: Modal GPU Glossary applies Little's Law to GPU inference. The connection between batch size and arithmetic intensity is a direct consequence: for a matrix-vector multiply (inference at B=1), weights are read once per output token, giving I ~ 1 FLOP/byte. At batch B, the same weight bytes produce B output tokens, giving I ~ B FLOP/byte.

citations.claim: ring all-reduce per-node data volume and time formula
citations.value: data per node = 2(N-1)/N × S; time ≈ 2(N-1)/N × S / B_link
citations.note: Preferred Networks blog derives this from the two-phase ring algorithm (reduce-scatter + all-gather), each phase sending (N-1)/N × S bytes per node. The result '2N(P-1)/P' in their notation equals 2(N-1)/N × S. Consistent with Baidu's ring-allreduce paper and bandwidth-optimality proofs (Rabenseifner 2004).
